threats.title: Supply-Chain Code Execution via Unguarded npm Lifecycle Hooks (Dockerfile:4)
threats.scenario: `Dockerfile:4` runs `npm install --production` without `--ignore-scripts`. Any dependency (or transitive dependency) that includes a `preinstall`, `install`, or `postinstall` lifecycle hook executes arbitrary shell commands inside the build container. With no `package-lock.json` (SC-002 violation), the resolved dependency graph is non-deterministic across builds — a dependency-confusion attack or a malicious version bump can inject a hook that exfiltrates build secrets, adds backdoor binaries to the image layer, or overwrites `server.js` before `COPY . .` is applied. The attacker targets the package registry namespace, not the running container.
threats.evidence_summary: `Dockerfile:4` calls `npm install --production` with no `--ignore-scripts` flag, and no `package-lock.json` is present in the repository to pin the resolved version graph.
threats.impact_description: Attacker-controlled code runs inside the build environment and is baked into the published container image, affecting every downstream deployment.
threats.controls_in_place: npm install uses `--production` to exclude devDependencies. No lockfile, no `--ignore-scripts`, no SCA scanner.
threats.mitigation_title: Add --ignore-scripts to npm install and commit a package-lock.json in Dockerfile

steps: Change `Dockerfile:4` to `RUN npm ci --omit=dev --ignore-scripts` (requires a committed `package-lock.json`).
Commit `package-lock.json` to the repository and add a CI step that fails the build when the lockfile is out of sync (`npm ci` exits non-zero when it is).
Add a `.npmrc` with `ignore-scripts=true` as defense-in-depth so `npm install` invocations elsewhere also skip hooks.
remediation.code_example:
# Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine@sha256:<digest>
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev --ignore-scripts
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

threats.title: Mutable Base Image Tag Allows Undetected Image Substitution (Dockerfile:1)
threats.scenario: `Dockerfile:1` specifies `FROM node:20-alpine` using a mutable floating tag. Docker Hub resolves this tag at build time to whatever image is currently published under that name. If the upstream `node:20-alpine` image is compromised, rotated, or poisoned via a registry supply-chain attack, the next `docker build` silently adopts the malicious image without any digest mismatch error. This satisfies the IF-007 FAIL from requirements-violations: no SHA256 digest pins the expected manifest. An attacker with registry write access (or a compromised Docker Hub account) can substitute the base image and execute code in every new build.
threats.evidence_summary: `Dockerfile:1` uses `FROM node:20-alpine` with no `@sha256:` digest, making the base image layer non-deterministic and unverifiable.
threats.impact_description: A malicious base image is incorporated into the production container image without detection, providing persistent code execution in all deployments built after the substitution.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no digest pinning, no image signing (Cosign/Notary) configured.
threats.mitigation_title: Pin base image to immutable SHA256 digest in Dockerfile

steps: Replace `FROM node:20-alpine` with `FROM node:20-alpine@sha256:<current-digest>` — obtain via `docker buildx imagetools inspect node:20-alpine`.
Add a Renovate or Dependabot `docker` datasource configuration to open PRs when a new Node 20 LTS Alpine image digest is published, keeping the pin current.
Optionally verify image signatures with `cosign verify node:20-alpine@sha256:<digest>` in CI before the build step.

threats.title: Build Context Leaks Sensitive Files Into Image Layer (Dockerfile:5)
threats.scenario: `Dockerfile:5` runs `COPY . .`, which copies the entire build context (the directory where `docker build` is invoked) into `/app`. No `.dockerignore` file exists in the repository. If the developer's working directory contains `.env`, `.env.local`, private key files (`*.pem`, `id_rsa`), or AWS/GCP credential files, these are baked into the image layer. Even if the files are removed in a later `RUN` instruction, they remain recoverable from the layer history via `docker image save` or a registry pull. Any user with `docker pull` access to the registry can read the secrets by inspecting the layer tarball.
threats.evidence_summary: No `.dockerignore` exists and `Dockerfile:5` copies the entire build context, exposing any local credential or configuration file present at build time as a permanent image layer.
threats.impact_description: Secrets embedded in the image layer are recoverable by any party with registry read access, including CI runners, all developers, and external attackers if the registry is public.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no .dockerignore, no multi-stage build to separate build and runtime contexts.
threats.mitigation_title: Add .dockerignore to exclude sensitive files from the Docker build context

steps: Create a `.dockerignore` file at the repository root excluding: `.env*`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, `.git/`, `node_modules/`, and any credential files.
Convert to a multi-stage build so build-time secrets never touch the final image layer — use `--mount=type=secret` (BuildKit) to inject secrets at build time without persisting them.
Audit existing published images for leaked secrets using `docker history --no-trunc <image>` and revoke any credentials found.

threats.title: Container Process Runs as Root Without USER Directive (Dockerfile)
threats.scenario: The `Dockerfile` contains no `USER` directive, so the Node.js process started by `CMD ["node", "server.js"]` runs as UID 0 (root) inside the container. This violates requirement IF-002. If an attacker achieves code execution through an application vulnerability (e.g., RCE via `eval()` or path traversal), they operate as root within the container. Root inside a default Docker container has near-full access to the container filesystem and can attempt container escape via kernel exploits, mounted volume abuse, or misconfigured `--privileged` flags added at runtime. The IF-002 FAIL evidence confirms: "Container runs as UID 0."
threats.evidence_summary: `Dockerfile` has no `USER` instruction before `CMD`, confirmed by direct inspection; the requirements-violations index records this as IF-002 FAIL.
threats.impact_description: Post-exploitation pivot from application RCE to container root, maximising the blast radius of any code execution vulnerability and enabling container escape attempts.
threats.controls_in_place: Docker namespace isolation provides partial containment. No additional security profiles (AppArmor, seccomp) are defined in the Dockerfile.
threats.mitigation_title: Add USER directive to run container process as non-root in Dockerfile

steps: Add `RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup` (Alpine syntax) to the Dockerfile before the `COPY` instructions.
Add `USER appuser` before the final `CMD` instruction so the Node.js process starts as a non-root user.

Verify the application can bind port 3000 as a non-root user (ports > 1024 require no special capability on Linux); if a privileged port is needed, use `setcap cap_net_bind_service` on the node binary instead of running as root.
remediation.code_example:
# Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev --ignore-scripts
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .
USER appuser
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

threats.title: No Resource Limits Defined — Container Can Exhaust Host Resources (Dockerfile)
threats.scenario: The `Dockerfile` defines no memory or CPU limits. No `ulimit` instructions are present, and there is no `docker-compose.yml` to enforce `deploy.resources.limits`. A single runaway request handler (e.g., a regex catastrophe, an infinite loop introduced by a supply-chain compromise, or a legitimate flood of requests) can consume all available host memory and CPU, causing the host and any co-located containers to become unresponsive. Because the process runs as root (see container-infra-004), even a `fork()` bomb would not be constrained by per-user process limits.
threats.evidence_summary: `Dockerfile` contains no `ulimit` or resource-limit directive; no `docker-compose.yml` was found in the repository to apply runtime constraints.
threats.impact_description: A single compromised or misbehaving container instance can render the entire host and co-located services unavailable.
threats.controls_in_place: None defined at the image level. Runtime enforcement depends entirely on undocumented deployment configuration.
threats.mitigation_title: Define memory and CPU resource limits in docker-compose.yml or container runtime configuration

steps: Add a `docker-compose.yml` (or update deployment manifests) with `deploy.resources.limits.memory` and `deploy.resources.limits.cpus` set to values appropriate for the application's load profile.
Set `--memory-swap` equal to `--memory` to prevent swap exhaustion.
Add a health check (`HEALTHCHECK` in the Dockerfile) so the orchestrator can restart the container if it becomes unresponsive.
remediation.code_example:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.50'
          memory: 256M
